narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and academic rankings to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 28.50, full-time student status, and school decile rankings of 2nd in Year 1 and 4th in Year 3—and calculated a 41% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your undergraduate GPA, and your Year 1 school decile ranking, each pulling the prediction in different directions compared to typical students. Most notably, your 4th-place decile ranking in Year 3—better than the average student's 5.53—strongly worked in your favor, pushing the model toward predicting bar exam success by showing solid improvement and performance relative to peers. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, though below the typical 3.21 average, still contributed positively to the prediction, suggesting it wasn't a severe drag despite being lower than most. On the other hand, your lower 2nd-place decile in Year 1—well below the average of 5.71—pushed against you, signaling early academic struggles that raised concerns about long-term bar readiness when weighed against the stronger later performance. Overall, while your later academic indicators gave you an edge over many applicants, the early weakness tipped the balance toward that concerning 41% failure risk. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 36.7 average, as it could significantly strengthen future applications, or focus on graduate-level courses to demonstrate upward trajectory in your deciles. Building a stronger narrative around your full-time commitment and any relevant experiences could also help, and reapplying after targeted improvements has succeeded for many in similar situations. With your middle-income background providing a stable foundation, you're well-positioned to address these gaps and succeed next time.
